default_creds.context: Hardcoded Supabase DEMO service_role JWT (iss:supabase-demo) committed in supabase/migrations/20240108234540_setup.sql at the delete_storage_object() function. This is the well-known default local-Supabase service-role key. If an operator exposes the Supabase Kong/REST gateway (default container supabase_kong_chatbotui:8000, host port 54321) without rotating it, this key grants full service_role (RLS-bypassing) DB+storage access. The README only instructs replacing it for HOSTED deployments; local-quickstart instances keep the demo key.

No shipped login credential, but config.toml enable_signup=true + enable_confirmations=false + empty EMAIL_WHITELIST means an exposed instance lets any visitor create their own account with no approval = effective default-credential equivalent for app access.

install_tell: git clone https://github.com/mckaywrigley/chatbot-ui.git && npm install && supabase start && cp .env.local.example .env.local && npm run chat   # local UI at http://localhost:3000, Supabase Studio at http://localhost:54323
misconfig_patterns: Exposing port 3000 to the internet with Supabase default config (open signup, no email confirmation, empty whitelist) -> unauthorized registration + LLMjacking
Co-exposing Supabase ports (54321/54322/54323) on the same public host as the frontend
Leaving the hardcoded demo service_role key in migration unrotated on a publicly reachable Supabase gateway
Setting OPENAI_API_KEY/etc as server env vars (intended to 'override globally for all users') on an instance that allows open signup = paying for anonymous strangers' inference
Following only the LOCAL quickstart (which never tells you to rotate the demo key or lock signups) and then exposing it

response_markers: X-Powered-By: Next.js header on / and /api/* routes
active_probe.false_positive_check: "Chatbot UI" as a title/phrase collides with thousands of unrelated chat apps and generic LLM front-ends, so title-only matching is a ~50%-rule trap. Disambiguate to THIS project by requiring the Mckay Wrigley fingerprint stack together: (1) the landing page renders the exact "Start Chatting" CTA link to /login plus the inline ChatbotUISVG component; (2) requesting / 302/308-redirects into a LOCALE-PREFIXED login path (/en/login, /fr/login etc.) because of next-i18n-router middleware -- generic chat UIs do not locale-prefix; (3) /manifest.json returns short_name AND name both exactly "Chatbot UI" with theme_color #000000 and a maskable /icon-512x512.png plus description 'Chabot UI PWA!'; (4) Next.js tells (X-Powered-By: Next.js, _next/static asset paths). Exclude the v1.0 'legacy' branch lineage (localStorage architecture, no /login, no Supabase) and unrelated WordPress/Oracle 'chatbot' products surfaced by keyword search."

deployment_tells: Port 3000 serving a Next.js app titled 'Chatbot UI' with a 'Start Chatting' -> /login landing
Companion Supabase stack ports nearby: 54321 (Kong/REST API), 54322 (Postgres), 54323 (Studio), 54324 (Inbucket mail catcher)
Docker container/network names containing 'chatbotui' (project_id='chatbotui' in config.toml -> supabase_kong_chatbotui, supabase_db_chatbotui, *_chatbotui_default network)
Often fronted by Vercel (hosted README path) -> x-vercel-id / vercel-edge headers, or self-hosted via ghcr.io/mckaywrigley/chatbot-ui image
PWA manifest at /manifest.json with description 'Chabot UI PWA!' (note the vendor's typo 'Chabot')
pivot_paths: If Supabase REST (54321) exposed: query PostgREST endpoints with the leaked demo service_role key for full RLS-bypass read of profiles/messages/files tables (READ-ONLY enumeration of schema/row counts only -- names are the finding, do not exfiltrate)
If Supabase Studio (54323) exposed: /project/default/editor gives a full DB GUI

If app self-signup open: register an account, then read your own workspace to confirm the instance is live and which LLM providers are wired (model dropdown reveals which provider keys the operator set)
Enumerate /api/username/available?username=X to probe account/username namespace without auth
Read /manifest.json + /_next/static/*/_buildManifest.js to fingerprint exact Next.js build and route set
GET / and inspect 302/308 Location for locale prefix to confirm the i18n middleware = this fork
vulnerabilities: No CVE or GHSA is assigned specifically to mckaywrigley/chatbot-ui as of 2026-06-12 (GitHub Advisory DB + repo Security tab show none; keyword 'chatbot-ui' results map to unrelated WordPress/Oracle products). Posture risk is configuration-class, not a tracked CVE.

Config-class exposure (not CVE): open self-signup default (config.toml enable_signup=true, enable_confirmations=false, empty EMAIL_WHITELIST) -> unauthorized account creation + LLMjacking of operator's server-side keys on any internet-exposed instance.
Config-class exposure (not CVE): hardcoded Supabase demo service_role JWT in migration 20240108234540_setup.sql -> full RLS-bypass DB access if Supabase REST gateway (54321) is exposed unrotated.

Inherited dependency surface: as a Next.js app, instances pin a Next.js version in package.json/package-lock.json; unrelated Next.js middleware/SSRF CVEs (e.g. CVE-2025-29927 auth-bypass class) may apply to stale forks but are not chatbot-ui-specific -- version-scope per deployed Next.js build, not assumed.
auth_evidence: Architecture is Next.js (port 3000) fronting Supabase Auth + Postgres-with-RLS; auth is INHERITED from Supabase, not built in-app. The web UI is login-gated: app/[locale]/page.tsx renders only a "Start Chatting" button linking to /login, and app/[locale]/layout.tsx wraps the app shell as `{session ? <GlobalState>{children}</GlobalState> : children}` so no usable UI renders without a session. The REST/chat/retrieval API routes are NOT unauthenticated: every model route calls getServerProfile() in lib/server/server-chat-helpers.ts, which runs `supabase.auth.getUser()` and `throw new Error("User not found")` when there is no valid Supabase session cookie. Data isolation is Supabase RLS (e.g. setup migration policy `TO authenticated USING (auth.uid()::text = owner_id::text)`). HOWEVER, the documented-quickstart Supabase config ships the access-restriction control OFF: supabase/config.toml sets `[auth] enable_signup = true`, `[auth.email] enable_signup = true`, and `[auth.email] enable_confirmations = false`; and .env.local.example leaves `EMAIL_DOMAIN_WHITELIST=` and `EMAIL_WHITELIST=` EMPTY. Net effect on a default exposed instance: any internet user can self-register an account with no email confirmation and immediately use the app — including spending the operator's server-side LLM API keys set via OPENAI_API_KEY/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env vars (which getServerProfile's addApiKeysToProfile injects globally for all users). The hardening lever (set EMAIL_WHITELIST / EMAIL_DOMAIN_WHITELIST, or disable signup in the Supabase dashboard per README "Configure Auth" step) exists but is off in the shipped defaults. Quoted: supabase/config.toml lines `enable_signup = true` / `enable_confirmations = false`; .env.local.example `EMAIL_DOMAIN_WHITELIST=` / `EMAIL_WHITELIST=`.
sensitive_assets: Upstream LLM provider API keys: OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GOOGLE_GEMINI_API_KEY, MISTRAL_API_KEY, GROQ_API_KEY, PERPLEXITY_API_KEY, OPENROUTER_API_KEY, AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY -- server-injected globally for all users via getServerProfile(); any self-registered user can burn them (LLMjacking)
Per-user API keys stored in the Supabase 'profiles' table (openai_api_key, anthropic_api_key, etc. columns)
Ingested RAG documents and their chunked embeddings: 'files', 'file_items' (vector store via pgvector), 'collections' tables -- the private documents users uploaded for retrieval
Custom assistant/agent definitions and system prompts: 'assistants', 'presets', 'prompts', 'tools' tables (business logic / proprietary prompts)
Workspace metadata and uploaded workspace images
Supabase Postgres directly (port 54322) and Supabase Studio admin GUI (port 54323, /project/default/editor) if those ports are exposed alongside 3000
